---
title: "Repo structure"
---

<div style="height: 2em;"></div>


## `.gitignore`

`.gitignore` is a plain text file that tells Git which files and folders to leave out of commits. Use it for files that are generated automatically or contain sensitive information. **It does not stop Git from tracking a file that was already committed.**

If you selected a `.gitignore` template when you first created your repo, it will already specify the usual suspects to ignore. You can amend it as needed.

+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+
| Rule                        | What it does                                                 | Example             |
+=============================+==============================================================+=====================+
| `#` at the start of a line  | Adds a comment; comments are skipped by Git                  | `# A comment`       |
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+
| File name                   | Ignores files with that name                                 | `.Rhistory`         |
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+
| `*` wildcard                | Ignores files with a matching extension or name pattern      | `*.xlsx`            |
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+
| Folder name ending in `/`   | Ignores that folder and everything inside it                 | `renv/library/`     |
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+
| Leading `/`                 | Ignores that directory, anchored at the project root         | `/data/`            |
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+
| `!` at the start of a line  | Exempts from previous rules, except if its parent is ignored | `!data/example.csv` |
+-----------------------------+--------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+

More rules can be found [here](https://git-scm.com/docs/gitignore).


## README

`README.md`, if present, is rendered on the landing page of your repo. Here is an example from the [PRISMA2020 Flow Diagram GitHub page](https://github.com/prisma-flowdiagram/PRISMA2020). `README.md` should help people understand and use your repo [@githubrepositories]:

- Purpose
- Motivation
- How to get started
- Where to get help
- Who maintains and contributes the project

`.md` means that `README.md` is a Markdown file. We will explore the syntax for Markdown more in the [next chapter](06_markup.qmd#markdown).


## LICENSE

Making your repo public lets others view and fork it, but without a license, you retain all rights to your project ("all rights reserved"), preventing reuse. Use [this guide](https://choosealicense.com/) to help find an appropriate license.

Your project may have dependencies, which themselves have licenses. Note the difference:

- **Copyright:** a legal right typically held by the author/contributors (being open source does not "give up" copyright -- you are just granting permission via the license)
- **Copyleft:** a condition that requires derived works to be licensed under the same or compatible license (if none is specified, then there is no obligation)

The MIT License is simple and permissive. By contrast, the GNU GPLv3 license is a strong copyleft license that requires people who distribute your project to keep it free. License conflicts can arise when software with incompatible licenses is combined:

- E.g., combining software under competing copyleft licenses
- E.g., combining software under non-commercial use into an open source project (this will prevent the project from being used commercially)

To add a license to your repo, you can generate one when you first create the repo. Otherwise, you can copy the license into a plain text file called `LICENSE` saved to your project root.


## Revised repo structure

Overall, a typical repo may look like this:

```{.markdown}
.
â”śâ”€â”€ analysis/
â”śâ”€â”€ data/
â”śâ”€â”€ docs/
â”śâ”€â”€ output/
â”śâ”€â”€ renv/              # To be discussed
â”śâ”€â”€ src/
â”śâ”€â”€ .Rprofile          # To be discussed
â”śâ”€â”€ .gitignore
â”śâ”€â”€ LICENSE
â”śâ”€â”€ README.md
â”śâ”€â”€ drugs_canada.RProj
â””â”€â”€ renv.lock          # To be discussed
```

The ones flagged by `#` will be discussed in a [later chapter on dependency management](08_renv.qmd).


::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
## References
:::
